task: I’m dealing with a stalled sale and need to decide what to do next after the file hit a snag in the paperwork. Can you help me map out a practical order for the remaining steps so I can keep the process from drifting?

turns.agent_response:
Yes. Use this order:

## 1) Identify the exact snag
Get the actual defect or rejection note:
- title issue
- recording issue
- signature / notarization issue
- legal description issue
- vesting / ownership issue
- permit / code issue
- lender condition issue

Don’t work from summaries.

## 2) Classify it
- **Blocker:** affects title, ownership, recording, insurability, or closing
- **Cleanup:** formatting, fees, attachments, exhibit order, clerical errors

If it changes ownership, legal description, or signing authority, treat it as a blocker.

## 3) Find the owner of the fix
Decide who can actually cure it:
- title company
- lender
- escrow
- attorney
- seller
- recorder
- county / permit office

## 4) Fix the controlling issue first
If the source document is wrong, fix the source.
If the filing package is wrong, correct the package.
If a missing affidavit or release cures it, get that first.

## 5) Rebuild one clean package
- same terminology
- same numbering
- no extra material unless it directly cures the defect

## 6) Get written confirmation if needed
Before resubmitting, confirm:
- the correction is acceptable
- the file can move
- no other hidden issue remains

## 7) Resubmit once
Send:
- corrected documents
- short cover note
- reference to the original defect
- what was changed

## 8) Keep the process moving
While that’s happening:
- hold the contractor at the last funded point if applicable
- don’t let the file drift into multiple parallel rewrites
- keep one person owning the update log

## Rule
Fix the thing that blocks the next approval, not the thing that is easiest to fix.

If you want, I can turn this into a **blocker / cleanup / owner / resubmit checklist**.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But it’s a little too generic.

I need the sequence tied to who actually has leverage at each point — title, escrow, lender, seller, recorder. And I’d want a hard split between what stops closing and what can be cleaned up after. Right now it reads like a nice checklist, but not enough for a stalled file.

Give me the order as:
1) blocker
2) owner
3) cure
4) proof in writing
5) resubmit
6) what to hold back until it clears

And if there’s a title defect, I want that separated from a pure recording error. Those don’t get handled the same way.

turns.agent_response:
Use this order:

## 1) Blocker
Separate it into one of two lanes:

### Title defect
- affects ownership
- vesting
- legal description
- insurability
- lien / release / encumbrance
- closing validity

### Recording error
- formatting
- fee issue
- acknowledgment / notarization placement
- exhibit order
- filing mechanics
- clerical rejection only

If it changes ownership, legal description, or signing authority, it’s a **title defect**, not a clerical issue.

## 2) Owner
Assign the person with leverage:

- **Title defect:** title company, seller, attorney, or escrow depending on who controls the cure
- **Recording error:** recorder / county filing office / escrow / attorney
- **Lender condition:** lender / draw reviewer / LO
- **Permit / code issue:** county / permit office / seller / contractor if it’s a work issue

## 3) Cure
Fix the controlling issue first:

- source document if the defect is in the source
- corrected filing set if it’s only the package
- release / affidavit / corrected legal description if that clears the block

Do not polish the package before the actual defect is cured.

## 4) Proof in writing
Get written confirmation that:
- the cure is acceptable
- the file can move
- the defect is resolved
- no additional condition is being added

If they won’t confirm it, you don’t know the file is clear.

## 5) Resubmit
Send one clean package:
- same numbering
- same terminology
- corrected item only
- no extra filler unless it directly cures the defect

## 6) What to hold back until it clears
Hold back:
- final closing
- final funding
- contractor overages
- noncritical work
- any side corrections that don’t affect the block

If it’s a title defect, do **not** treat it like a recording cleanup.  
If it’s a pure recording error, don’t rebuild the whole deal.

## Rule
- **Title defect:** cure first, then resubmit
- **Recording error:** correct package, then resubmit
- **No written proof:** not cleared
- **No clear owner:** not moving

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page blocker / owner / cure / proof / resubmit sheet**.

user_simulator.text:
Better. That’s the right split.

What I needed was the leverage map, and you gave it:
- title defect = title company / seller / attorney / escrow, depending on who can actually cure it
- recording error = recorder / county / escrow / attorney
- lender condition = lender
- permit/code = county or seller, sometimes contractor if it’s a real work fix

And the hard line is right too:
- title defect blocks closing
- pure recording error is a cleanup unless it affects validity

The part I’d use as the operating sequence is basically:
1) identify whether it’s title defect or recording error
2) assign the owner with actual leverage
3) cure the blocking issue first
4) get written confirmation it’s accepted
5) resubmit one clean package
6) hold back final funding / closing / noncritical spend until it clears

That’s usable. Not fluffy. If you want, the next thing would be turning it into a tighter decision tree with title / escrow / lender / seller / recorder as the branches.
